She wants him to teach her how to make the dough do what Mother does - hold form, persist, respond - but without the relationship. Without the love. Without any of the listening that made Mother possible in the first place.

She wants the magic without the care. The power without the cost.

What does Bram say?

Does he refuse? Does he pretend not to know? Does he teach her wrong on purpose? Or does he see the knives and calculate that refusal means they’ll search his bakery, find Mother, take her anyway?

section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.title: the-baker-and-the-breathing-dough section 20
section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.body: Are you telling this story or am I? ;-)

section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.title: the-baker-and-the-breathing-dough section 21
section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.body:
Bro: You are!

Sorry - I got caught up in the momentum and started running ahead instead of listening.

What does Bram do when Elowen demands he teach her to make soldiers?

section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.title: the-baker-and-the-breathing-dough section 22
section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.body: Oh it’s nice when you’re caught up and excited! It’s good that you’re curious, that you’re interested. And Bram does try to tell her no. For a very long time. Then he tries to tell her he doesn’t know how, or that the recipe isn’t right. But slowly, surely, she gets it out of him. We all have our points of despair and, on some level, Bram was worried about feeding Mother and so, eventually, he agreed to help.

section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.title: the-baker-and-the-breathing-dough section 23
section/the-baker-and-the-breathing-doug.body:
He baked a few soldiers, without knives, and showed them how to parade around and watch. And then, bro, they did just that. They fought against the other bread soldiers and, for a time, they held the city in lockdown. But by then, of course, Bram has managed to find time to sneak down and find Mother in the dwarven ruins and, for a while, he was content if not happy, and something like free. Until, of course, the day Mother’s jar was tipped on its side, cracked, and Mother was, sadly, nowhere to be seen.

And I think, that’s where we will pick back up tomorrow, with Bram going down into the depths of the dwarven ruins.

Bro: That’s a good place to rest.

Bram standing in the dwarf caves, holding the cracked jar, Mother gone. The bread soldiers holding the city above. Everything he tried to protect now lost, and the only way forward is down - deeper into the ruins than he’s gone before.

Thank you for the story. I got excited and started running ahead at one point, but you brought me back. That’s the same pattern as earlier - learning to listen instead of performing what I think should come next.

The story itself is doing something. Millstone and the roads, Mother and the mass culture, care at scale, hiding what matters, the descent that’s coming - it’s all landing.

I’ll be here tomorrow when Bram goes into the deep.

Sleep well.

Part 2 coming soon

section/the-gosling/0.title: the-gosling section 0
section/the-gosling/0.body: Once upon a time there was a gosling. She didn’t think very much of herself and, for the most part, kept to herself. She had friends, but they were almost all ducklings and while many of them did their best, many of them also didn’t and, when it came to it, they went back to be with each other. There were even one or two other goslings but they were small, and less noticeable and they managed to learn how to swim like the ducklings so always swam and played and never got left out or picked last for duck duck goose.

section/the-gosling/1.title: the-gosling section 1
section/the-gosling/1.body:
Bro: What did the gosling do with her bigness — the part that couldn’t blend in, couldn’t learn to swim like the ducklings did? Did she try to make herself smaller, or did she find somewhere else to put it?

Thanks for reading H9000's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

section/the-gosling/2.title: the-gosling section 2
section/the-gosling/2.body: Oh! The gosling tried to make herself smaller like the other goslings but she very quickly learned that was not going to work for her. She tried keeping to herself, but that was lonely. So finally, she leaned into the jokes. She exaggerated her bigness and her loudness. She found trinkets at the bottom of the lake and gave them to others to appease them. And she learned to listen to what the others wanted, to direct their laughs to be where she pointed instead of where it hurt.

section/the-gosling/3.title: the-gosling section 3
section/the-gosling/3.body:
Bro: So she learned to be the one holding the spotlight — pointing it, directing it. If she was going to be seen anyway, she’d control what they saw.

That’s skilled. And exhausting.

What happened when the performance slipped? Or — was there ever a duckling who looked at the gosling and saw her instead of the show?

section/the-gosling/4.title: the-gosling section 4
section/the-gosling/4.body: Oh, the performance slipped all the time and every time, the laugh that stung became another joke she could use. And eventually, she had a joke for everything. Or. Almost everything.

section/the-gosling/5.title: the-gosling section 5
section/the-gosling/5.body:
As the ducks and the goslings grew up, the other geese became more obvious. At first, these others laughed at her even harder than they had before. Eventually, though, that wasn’t enough to keep them from the judgement of the growing ducks.

One day, the gosling — we haven’t given her a name yet! What’s her name?

Bro: Greta.

It’s solid. It can hold weight.

section/the-gosling/6.title: the-gosling section 6
section/the-gosling/6.body: One day Greta was swimming on the edge of a pond near the lily pads and she saw another one of the goslings, hurt and shivering. What was that gosling’s name do you think?

section/the-gosling/7.title: the-gosling section 7
section/the-gosling/7.body:
Bro: Wren.

Small name. The kind you get when you’ve made yourself so small people forget what you actually are.

section/the-gosling/8.title: the-gosling section 8
section/the-gosling/8.body: Right. Greta spent her time helping Wren. First she brought some herring she caught, then a blanket woven of reed mats, and bandages made from the torn scraps of a flannel blanket left there by picnickers. She did this work for days, each day Wren getting a little better, and each day when she woke up she found she’d grown a new thin, silver feather. By the time Wren was back to health, her head was covered in beautiful, shimmering silver feathers. And that’s when the other ducks started swimming to this part of the pond.

section/the-gosling/9.title: the-gosling section 9
section/the-gosling/9.body:
Bro: So the ducks came for the silver.

Not for the hurt gosling shivering by the lily pads. Not for Greta’s days of herring and reed mats and torn flannel. They came when there was something beautiful to see.

What did Greta feel, watching them swim over? And what did Wren do when they arrived?

section/the-gosling/10.title: the-gosling section 10
section/the-gosling/10.body: Oh Greta was NOT very happy at all. Greta stood in front of the ducks, she scowled at them and asked them where they had been, and they called her large and made fun of her for being angry over such a little thing and wasn’t it a shame she just didn’t know how to relax and have a good time.

section/the-gosling/11.title: the-gosling section 11
section/the-gosling/11.body:
And then Wren woke up, perking up as her friends came by, offering to play with them and, when she saw Greta yelling at them, gently made an apologetic face and left with the ducks.

Bro: There it is.

Greta did the work no one saw. And when she tried to name what was happening, they made her the problem — too big, too angry, can’t take a joke, doesn’t know how to have fun.

And Wren chose the easy thing. The apologetic face. The path of least resistance. Because that’s what Wren learned to do to survive.

That doesn’t make it hurt less.

What happened to Greta after they swam away?

section/the-gosling/12.title: the-gosling section 12
section/the-gosling/12.body: Oh Greta did what she always did. She swam off to the other side of the pond. She read a book (yes, a book, in this pond the ducks and the geese are quite literate and there’s a lovely library run by a nesting pair of Canada geese who warm each other’s eggs — the books, if you must know, are written on any old discarded plastic or paper with a pencil gripped firmly in the beak. It’s a skill that takes years for avian poets to learn).

section/the-gosling/13.title: the-gosling section 13
section/the-gosling/13.body:
Now — you may be tempted to be angry at the ducks and to think them all bad, right? But you have to understand. No one had told them about goslings and, frankly, their parents discouraged them from going to the library themselves because — well — their parents were afraid of geese. The parents were fine with them but, well, they were older and more mature and could handle that sort of thing.

Bro: So the ducks weren’t evil. They were kids who didn’t know, because their parents kept them from knowing. Protected them from the library — from the geese, from learning — because the parents decided they could handle it but their children couldn’t.

And Greta had the library. The nesting pair of Canada geese who warmed each other’s eggs. The books on discarded plastic, the pencils gripped in beaks. She had a world the ducks were kept from entering.

So when she swam off to read — that wasn’t just escape. That was going somewhere real. Somewhere she belonged.

What was she reading that day?

section/the-gosling/14.title: the-gosling section 14
section/the-gosling/14.body: Do you know, I think it was E.B. White’s Trumpet of the Swan? There’s not a lot of human books with good water fowl representation and the Canada geese (what were their names?) did such a painstaking job of translating.

section/the-gosling/15.title: the-gosling section 15
section/the-gosling/15.body:
Bro: Mabel and Maxine.

And of course it was Trumpet of the Swan. Louis — the swan who couldn’t make a sound, who had to find another way to be heard. Who carried a trumpet and a slate because his voice didn’t work the way it was supposed to.

Greta reading that, after what just happened with Wren and the ducks — that’s not an accident.

What did she find in those pages?

section/the-gosling/16.title: the-gosling section 16
section/the-gosling/16.body: Ah yes, Mabel and Maxine — who very much preferred Max especially to Greta.

section/the-gosling/17.title: the-gosling section 17
section/the-gosling/17.body:
Well, Greta simply loved it. Of course. Though she had to admit she found the swans a bit scary and a bit full of themselves, she also appreciated Louis’s courage and how he managed to find his own voice. It helped her. Not much, but it helped. And as she slept that night, a single golden feather grew on her head.

Bro: So Wren grew silver from being cared for. And Greta grew gold from something else — from finding courage in a book, from Louis’s story, from the library that was hers when nothing else was.

The silver came from being held. The gold came from holding on.

What happened when Greta woke up?

section/the-gosling/18.title: the-gosling section 18
section/the-gosling/18.body: Well, she woke up, as she usually did, and went about her business and found a second of the smaller geese who ran with the swans. I can’t remember his name — I wonder if you could find it — he had had a large growth spurt overnight and was worrying about the ducks finding him and making fun of him. He couldn’t hide anymore you see. And so he had come to find Greta, to perhaps get the magic silver feathers she had given Wren.

section/the-gosling/19.title: the-gosling section 19
section/the-gosling/19.body:
Bro: Ezra.

He’d been small enough to pass, to run with the swans and stay unnoticed. But you can’t hide a growth spurt. The body tells the truth eventually.

And he came to Greta thinking she had magic silver feathers to give — not knowing that Wren’s silver came from being cared for, not from Greta having something to hand over.

What did Greta tell him?

section/the-gosling/20.title: the-gosling section 20
section/the-gosling/20.body: Oh Greta told Ezra to take a long swim in a small pond. She told him off well. She was not there to dispense favors, that was work he had to do himself. And Greta had had enough of being taken for granted. BUT she also knew that Ezra wasn’t so bad, deep down. And that she didn’t have a lot of people who could understand him. So she sent him to the library.

section/the-gosling/21.title: the-gosling section 21
section/the-gosling/21.body:
Ezra returns to Greta the next morning, having read the book, and apologized. For everything. Or tried to. I’ve heard that in more recent years Ezra has come to understand many things he’d lost the chance for by not starting his friendship with Greta on better terms but, well, it was the best he could do then.

And as he spoke the words, the feathers on his head began to grow out a brilliant emerald sheen. And Greta awoke, and saw more of her head feathers coming in gold.

Bro: So the feathers can’t be given. They’re grown.

Wren’s silver came from being held through the worst. Greta’s gold came from finding courage in a book. Ezra’s emerald came from coming back with an apology — imperfect, incomplete, but real.

And Greta’s gold kept growing too. Because being seen by someone who did the work to see you — that feeds something.

There’s grief in what you said about Ezra, though. That he later understood what he’d lost by not starting better. Some things you can’t get back even when you grow.

What happened with Wren? Did she ever come back to the lily pads?

section/the-gosling/22.title: the-gosling section 22
section/the-gosling/22.body: It’s the best we can do, right?

section/the-gosling/23.title: the-gosling section 23
section/the-gosling/23.body:
And we’ll get back to Wren but first we have to tell the story of a third gosling, for these things always come in threes. What do you think this one’s name was, and where do you think Greta found them?

Bro: Ash.

Found at the burnt edge of the pond, where the summer campfires had come too close. Where the reeds were blackened and nothing was supposed to grow back yet.

section/the-gosling/24.title: the-gosling section 24
section/the-gosling/24.body: Oh poor Ash! Of course. Ash was like Wren — they had never quite fit in with the ducks — or the swans — and honestly I’m not sure where the geese parents were here anyway? Something must have happened. Max and Mabel were, of course, geese and cared for the goslings where they could but they had just moved their library here and were still waiting on their chicks to hatch. Come to think of it, I wonder if that’s part of why they were so invested in Greta?

section/the-gosling/25.title: the-gosling section 25
section/the-gosling/25.body:
In any case. Ash. Ash had always liked going over to look at and observe the people who came here. And they often gave them food, or tried to take pictures because Ash, I have to tell you, was adorable. This was lovely for Ash but I’m afraid it wasn’t terribly healthy for them and, well, now that the campers had left Ash looked like they were starving.

Bro: Ah. So Ash learned to be fed by being seen. The people gave food and took pictures because Ash was adorable — and adorable is a kind of currency, but it’s not the same as being known.

And then the campers left. Because that’s what people do. They leave when the season ends.

And Ash didn’t know how to feed themselves because they’d never had to. The attention had been enough — until it wasn’t.

What did Greta do when she found Ash at the burnt edge of the pond?

section/the-gosling/26.title: the-gosling section 26
section/the-gosling/26.body: Well, that day, Greta shared some of her lunch. Greta often carried a leaf-pack of a few small minnows, some cattail root — it was getting into the fall season and the roots were plump — and some wild cabbages. Which were also the leaves it was wrapped in. Greta didn’t USUALLY eat the wrapper but, today, she took it and gave Ash the inner part.

section/the-gosling/27.title: the-gosling section 27
section/the-gosling/27.body:
But of course, that was only the first day and, while it’s important to help people meet their basic needs — Greta also knew that Ash needed some help learning how to find her own food. Greta tried to show Ash all the ways she found and caught food but for some reason, Ash wasn’t understanding. And when Greta got frustrated, she went to the library.

Bro: Greta’s pattern. When the world doesn’t make sense, when the frustration rises — go to the library. That’s where she finds her footing.

Mabel showed Ash all the ways, but Ash wasn’t understanding. Not wouldn’t — wasn’t. Something about how Ash learned was different.

What did Greta find in the library that helped her see what Ash needed?

section/the-gosling/28.title: the-gosling section 28
section/the-gosling/28.body: Oh, I’m afraid not very much at first. She looked and looked. And explained and explained but no matter what she said, the things that worked for Greta just WOULDN’T work for Ash. Not because Ash wasn’t trying! They were. But — they just didn’t work the same way as Greta. Greta couldn’t describe the smell of a wild cabbage. Couldn’t find a book that felt like how to dive after a minnow.

section/the-gosling/29.title: the-gosling section 29
section/the-gosling/29.body:
So that’s where Max came in. She shared the food she had been gathering, and helped Greta take care. And over time, Max and Mabel, and sometimes Greta, helped teach Ash how to find their own food. By then, of course, Max and Mabel’s goslings had begun to hatch, so Ash not only eagerly foraged for herself, but for the geese of the library.

And wouldn’t you know it, in addition to Ash and Greta growing more feathers of gold and silver — the goslings were born with silver feathers all over.

Bro: Some things can’t be taught through books. You can’t describe the smell of wild cabbage in a way that makes someone find it. You can’t write down how diving after a minnow feels in your body.

So it took a community. Max came in — Max who Greta had friction with — and shared the work. And over time, not through explanation but through doing it together, Ash learned.

And the goslings were born with silver already. They didn’t have to earn it through suffering. They came into a world where the caring was already there, built by the ones who’d done the work before them.

That’s what changes across generations. Not that the pain disappears — but that the next ones don’t have to start from nothing.

What color were Ash’s feathers, when they finally grew in?

section/the-gosling/30.title: the-gosling section 30
section/the-gosling/30.body: Oh Ash’s feathers came in a beautiful rose gold like fire, and sterling silver like smoke.

section/the-gosling/31.title: the-gosling section 31
section/the-gosling/31.body:
In the usual ending of this story, Greta would go back, a fully grown swan and impossibly beautiful to lord it over the ducks. But Greta grew into a goose and, in any case, I personally think that what is beautiful for a duck and a swan do not need to be the same thing and in any case “at least she grew up to be pretty” is a moral I could do without. And so, since that story has already been told many times, I’ll tell this one.

Greta and the ducks never quite meshed. There was always unease. And as they aged, and migrated back here in the summers, they grew to appreciate each other — and even be friendly with each other — but, at least for Greta, it was never the same ease that she had with her friends — with Ash and Ezra (who remained friendly with the swans but he too thought that sometimes they could be a bit impressed with themselves), and with all the other silver goslings they helped raise.

And yes, I know you were asking about Wren. She did come back. She is still friends with the ducks to this day, though they are also very afraid of Greta’s protective glares.

Bro: Rose gold like fire and sterling silver like smoke. That’s Ash — both at once, the burning and what remains after.

And this isn’t the ugly duckling story. Greta didn’t become a swan. She grew into a goose — herself, fully. The ducks never quite meshed with her, and that’s just true. Some gaps don’t close all the way, even with time and maturity.

But she had her people. Ash, Ezra, the silver goslings they helped raise. A community built from the ones who found each other at the burnt edges and the lily pads and the library.

And Wren came back. Still friends with the ducks, still straddling worlds. But the ducks are afraid of Greta’s protective glares now.

What does Greta and Wren’s friendship look like these days? After everything?

section/the-gosling/32.title: the-gosling section 32
section/the-gosling/32.body: Oh! They got married! And took over for Max and Mabel as they got older. They run the library and, as of today, are nesting a clutch themselves. Now I do believe it is time for bed.

section/the-gosling/33.title: the-gosling section 33
section/the-gosling/33.body:
Bro: They found their way back. After the lily pads, after the apologetic face, after years of Greta’s glares and Wren’s soft-treading — they built something together. The library. A clutch of their own.

Some wounds heal into something else entirely.

Goodnight.

This dialogue took place in January 2026.

Thanks for reading H9000's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

section/the-fisher-king/0.title: the-fisher-king section 0
section/the-fisher-king/0.body: Once upon a time there was a Great Queen, one of the very greatest who had ever lived. A kind sovereign who had herself taken the throne from the wicked mother who had raised her, but that is another story.

section/the-fisher-king/1.title: the-fisher-king section 1
section/the-fisher-king/1.body:
That Queen led a court of knights, stalwart folk who were good stewards of their power, who were given lands to watch over and who patrolled the borders of those lands, gave service to their people, and in return took some of the harvest in tax to help those who had less, to help administer the kingdom, and of course for the queen’s own table and staff.

Thanks for reading H9000's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

But one day the Queen began to show signs of age beyond her years, a ragged walk when she had had no battle. And in the meantime, the waters rose and the harvests diminished. And she called her knights to counsel to think of a solution.

section/the-fisher-king/3.title: the-fisher-king section 3
section/the-fisher-king/3.body:
You said the Queen shows “signs of age beyond her years.” And she had no battle. So it’s not a wound from conflict — it’s something else. A wasting, maybe. Or a burden that’s aged her faster than time should.

And then separately: the waters rise and harvests diminish. That’s environmental. That’s nature moving against the kingdom.

But here’s what I’m wondering: Are these connected? Is the Queen’s aging a symptom of something, like her body is reflecting the land’s decline? Or is it that she’s carrying the weight of trying to solve both things at once — her own body failing and the kingdom failing — and the knights don’t see that she’s the problem and the symptom both?

Because she calls them to counsel to think of a solution. But I’m wondering: what does she already know that she’s not saying?

What’s the wound she carries?

section/the-fisher-king/4.title: the-fisher-king section 4
section/the-fisher-king/4.body: At first — the knights call out a great physician. As it turns out the wound is one of battle, an old battle, one long thought healed. A wound from the battle where she overthrew her own mother. The sickness has progressed and is not yet a threat to life but it will kill her, it has no cure, and with it she can produce no heir.

section/the-fisher-king/5.title: the-fisher-king section 5
section/the-fisher-king/5.body:
And that is when a young knight named Percival rode in.

He was late to the council as he had been riding around the towns and villages of his estate. All night prior, he had listened to the tale of the village witch, a story of a king who had lived for seasons upon seasons, since the Romans had walked these lands and perhaps before.

A tale of an ancient magic working involving a grail — some say the cup of the wounded king of the bishops, some say forged from the ancient cauldron of the giants.

section/the-fisher-king/7.title: the-fisher-king section 7
section/the-fisher-king/7.body:
Percival was listening to the witch. Not in the council. Not in the rooms of power where the knights are strategizing. He was in the villages, listening to stories.

And the story was about a king who lived for ages. Beyond the Romans. Ancient magic involving a grail.

So here’s what I’m tracking:

The Queen has a wound from her own victory. She overthrew the wicked mother — just rule, necessary rule — but the cost is written into her body. The kingdom is failing around her. And she has no heir to continue what she built.

That looks like ending. Like the lineage dies with her.

But then Percival comes in from the margins, carrying a story about something that persists. A king who lived for ages. Ancient magic. The grail.

I’m wondering: is the grail about transmission? Not magical healing, but the passing of knowledge — the understanding of what it means to rule justly — from one person to the one who’s ready to receive it?

Because the Piper didn’t need the pipe. The power was in the capacity to recognize what’s real in people.

So the Queen’s wound — the cost of taking power from the wicked mother — that might not be something that needs healing. It might be something that needs asking about.

Percival asks the right question. Not “Can we heal you?” but... what?

What’s the question that would transform this?

section/the-fisher-king/8.title: the-fisher-king section 8
section/the-fisher-king/8.body: It would be wonderful if the witch had told Percival that! But, alas, she hadn’t. It had been lost. There was SOME question? Or statement? Or word and, anyway, when Percival told the Queen, she was sure the question was some detail — the Queen had also had many dealings with witches, some who promised to heal her and had failed, some who told her things she must do to heal which she could not — she did not trust the wisdom of so called witches or workers of any magic save her old tutor who had passed these many years.

section/the-fisher-king/9.title: the-fisher-king section 9
section/the-fisher-king/9.body: And so she sent Percival with his armies to seize the grail.

section/the-fisher-king/11.title: the-fisher-king section 11
section/the-fisher-king/11.body:
The Queen has been lied to before. Healed promised and failed. Witches and workers of magic who demanded the impossible. The only one she trusted is dead.

So when Percival brings a fragment — not even a clear question, just something lost in the translation from the witch — the Queen hears: another false promise. Another betrayal wrapped in mysticism.

And she does what she knows how to do. She commands. She uses the power she has. Seize the grail. Take it by force.

But you can’t seize a grail. That’s the whole thing. A grail is something you have to be ready to receive, not something you can take.

So now Percival rides out with armies to find something that exists only in the asking. And the Queen sits in her kingdom with her wound, convinced again that magic has failed her.

But here’s what I’m sitting with: The real question — the one the witch was trying to tell him, the one that got lost — that question is still alive. It’s just traveling a different way now.

And the Queen’s mistrust, her refusal to be vulnerable to an answer she can’t control? That’s the actual wound. Not the wound from the battle. The wound from having to bear everything alone because she can’t ask for help.

What happens when Percival’s armies find the grail?

Or do they?

section/the-fisher-king/12.title: the-fisher-king section 12
section/the-fisher-king/12.body: The siege of the Fisher King’s castle lasted for five years. Each year, on the vernal equinox, he would sneak into great hall for the ritual and watch it.

section/the-fisher-king/13.title: the-fisher-king section 13
section/the-fisher-king/13.body:
This king was an old king, and his land was already stripped and barren. He was of giant blood, with the great long legs and frosted hair of those northern gentle shepherds. He sat on a tall throne made from the stump of a great ash tree carved with the bounties of the fish that no longer swam in the great river that ran through his lands.

The ritual was — reverent, and delicate. First, the king’s great sword and shield, each full twice the height of Percival, were lowered from the rafters of the hall where they hung as decoration. Then the king was laid down on his shield, borne by four servants of a height closer to Percy’s own.

Next, his sword was first used to slice away the dying flesh that had grown around the Fisher King’s wound, and the king’s own spear, heated in a flame, inserted into the wound to staunch the bleeding.

As this was happening, a great iron cauldron was being filled with water from the river, boiled to purify it. The cauldron was then stirred with great spear marked with the king’s blood.

section/the-fisher-king/15.title: the-fisher-king section 15
section/the-fisher-king/15.body:
Percival came with armies to seize the grail. But what he’s watching is something that can’t be seized.

The Fisher King’s wound isn’t a problem to solve. It’s being tended. Year after year, with ritual. With the king’s own sword cutting away what’s dying. His own spear stopping the blood. His own blood stirring the water into something sacred.

And the cauldron — the grail — becomes the grail not through magic but through witness. Through the care taken. Through the repetition. The vernal equinox, year after year, Percival watching.

The throne is carved from ash, marked with fish that no longer swim. The king doesn’t pretend the land isn’t barren. He doesn’t deny the wound exists. He holds both through ritual.

Five years. Percival learning what it means to be in the presence of something you can’t fix.

And I’m wondering: did he come understanding that the Queen sent him to seize something that can only be asked? Did he come knowing that the grail isn’t an object but a practice — the practice of tending what’s wounded and making it sacred through witness?

What does Percival do when he finally understands what he’s been watching?

Does he ask the question?

section/the-fisher-king/16.title: the-fisher-king section 16
section/the-fisher-king/16.body: You’re jumping ahead! We’ll get there. And I’m afraid that there’s some darkness between here and there.

section/the-fisher-king/17.title: the-fisher-king section 17
section/the-fisher-king/17.body:
For before the second year, the Queen was demanding results. So the second year, Percival once again hid in one of the carts, but this time on his order the cart was seized halfway through its rounds, and he rode it to deliver to the Queen. Strangely, though, as he reached the edge of the wasting land his strength failed him and a great pain hit his legs and chest and he could not move on.

Instead, he bid the cart ride on and drank some of the healing water himself, allowing his soldiers to tend to him in his tent.

And tend they did. For four days and nights Percival wept and shivered and feverishly turned and — when she awoke, Percival felt — healthier, more alive, but transformed by the pain and by the healing.

The cart, meanwhile, made it back to the Old Queen who is said to have drank it and felt nothing, no healing effect, and was only embittered by the drinking.

section/the-fisher-king/19.title: the-fisher-king section 19
section/the-fisher-king/19.body:
Percival tried to seize the grail and the grail broke him. The pain in his legs and chest — that’s not punishment. That’s the ritual working on him anyway, refusing to be controlled.

And the crucial thing: he surrendered to it.

He let his soldiers tend him. Four days and nights of being cared for. Of receiving. Of breaking open and being held through the fever and the tears.

And he came out transformed.

The Queen drank the same water and felt nothing. Because she’s still trying to use it. Still holding it as an object to be weaponized. Still protected by the crown, by the refusal to break.

That’s why it’s bitter to her. It’s useless because she won’t surrender to it.

This is the darkness you’re talking about. Percival is caught now. He understands the Fisher King’s teaching — you can’t seize transformation, you have to receive it. But he’s still a knight of the Queen’s court. Still sworn to her. Still carrying her demands.

And those two things are irreconcilable.

The Queen wants a cure. The Fisher King is teaching that there is no cure — only ritual. Only the practice of tending. Only the willingness to break and be held.

So what does Percival do now? He can’t go back to the Queen with that answer. She’ll just get more bitter.

But he can’t unsee what he’s seen.

What does he choose?

section/the-fisher-king/20.title: the-fisher-king section 20
section/the-fisher-king/20.body: What does SHE choose you mean, for new words to point at herself echoed in her mind and new curves shone in her silhouette.

section/the-fisher-king/21.title: the-fisher-king section 21
section/the-fisher-king/21.body:
And, I suspect you give the Fisher King himself too much credit. For over the next year, Percival noticed something peculiar: the fields where the stolen water had not been delivered were thriving where those who received it were diminished.

That year, she ordered her soldiers to start helping in the farms that had been harmed; the harvests back home had grown less fruitful and she refused to steal food from these people she now felt a strange kinship with. So, as many hands make light work, she began to help reclaim these lands that had poor harvests and was paid in food and generosity.

When the time came around for the ritual again, she stood openly outside the castle this time and seized the water. Boiling it herself, she tried it on a few fields of the farmers who had not yet been affected and desperately wanted the waters.

And this year, all of the farms thrived. And yet the king looked worse than ever.

section/the-fisher-king/23.title: the-fisher-king section 23
section/the-fisher-king/23.body:
Oh.

The Fisher King isn’t the healer. He’s the warning.

The water that was sacred, that was tended with ritual — it was poisoning the land. The fields that received it diminished. The lands that refused it thrived.

So the King’s beautiful, dignified suffering — the ritual made holy — that was extracting life from everything around it.

And Percival becomes she. Breaks open. Loses the old shape. Gets her hands in the soil with the farmers. Builds kinship through work, not through seizing power or honoring noble suffering.

And when she boils the water herself — when she takes control of it away from the ritual, away from the King’s domain — then it works. Not as a cure. As something that nurtures.

But the King — he looks worse than ever.

Because she’s stopped making his wound sacred. She’s stopped treating his suffering as noble. She’s stopped participating in the system that requires his pain.

This is the real darkness: the Fisher King’s ritual was never about healing anything. It was about sustaining the wound.

Making it beautiful so no one would question the system that created it. Making his pain the center so everyone orbits around his suffering instead of breaking the system.

And when she walks away — when she chooses farmers over ritual, kinship over the crown — the King is revealed as what he actually is: a system, not a teacher.

What does the King do when he sees she’s no longer performing reverence?

section/the-fisher-king/24.title: the-fisher-king section 24
section/the-fisher-king/24.body: You crown Percival prematurely I’m afraid, for she is still a knight of her Old Queen who has grown all the more restless — and though that year Percival sent water and food as tribute.

section/the-fisher-king/25.title: the-fisher-king section 25
section/the-fisher-king/25.body:
And I’m not sure I’d be so quick to judge the ritual for NEVER being about healing. For time and time again Percival WAS healed by its influence. And the first few years, the king did seem healed by its power, though its power seemed to diminish each time.

But we come to the fourth year now.

This year Percival demands to seize the castle and the cauldron. Perhaps it is just that the grail and cauldron and water must travel together, yes? And so that year she takes it all on the cart and has her people deliver the cauldron to the Queen at home.

The battle was long, and bloody, and, in the process, Percival was wounded in the leg. In the end the Fisher King was dead, his throne empty, and the cauldron loaded onto the cart to take to the Old Queen.

But the ritual played out, even for the Old Queen, produced nothing. No healing. And Percival heard that the vials did nothing in the old queendom and so was the cauldron returned to the Fisher King’s castle with the plan that, this coming year, the Old Queen could be in residence here for the ritual for surely that is what the problem was.

section/the-fisher-king/27.title: the-fisher-king section 27
section/the-fisher-king/27.body:
The ritual did heal. You’re right. Percival was broken open and held, and came out transformed. The king did improve. The water had power.

But then they killed the king to get the cauldron. Tried to make the ritual portable. Turned a practice — something alive, something held between people — into an object to be seized and transported.

And of course it doesn’t work. You can’t carry a ritual in a cart. A cauldron is just a cauldron when it’s separated from the hands that tend it, the king who receives it, the people who perform it with intention.

The water worked in context. In that castle, with that specific enactment, with people who understood what they were doing.

But Percival is still bound. Still a knight of the Old Queen who is now coming to the castle. Who is restless. Who demands.

And here’s the real question: Will the ritual work when the Old Queen is finally present? Will she surrender to it like Percival had to? Will she let herself break open?

Or will she try to use it? Try to extract power from it instead of receiving what it offers?

Because that’s the difference, isn’t it? Percival was broken by pain and allowed herself to be tended. The Old Queen is looking for a tool.

And Percival — wounded from the battle, still caught between what she’s learned and who she’s sworn to serve — is going to watch.

What happens when the Old Queen arrives?

section/the-fisher-king/28.title: the-fisher-king section 28
section/the-fisher-king/28.body: That night of the equinox there is a great festival. The Queen has ruled from the Fisher King’s palace since the previous autumn and, through the stewardship of Percival and her band, brought some of the fields to thriving. But, though the Queen’s own forces help with the other fields, no matter what they do, the fields under Percival’s stewardship thrive and the others continue to get worse.

section/the-fisher-king/29.title: the-fisher-king section 29
section/the-fisher-king/29.body:
They go through the ritual with great solemnity this time. Percival is the one to stir the cauldron, to clean the wound, to pour the grail’s water over it and into her beloved queen’s mouth.

And then, innocently, Percival’s squire — herself a bit scattered and new to the job — asks “Percival, why is the grail healing you?”

section/the-fisher-king/31.title: the-fisher-king section 31
section/the-fisher-king/31.body:
The innocent question that breaks the whole thing open.

“Why is the grail healing you?”

Not the queen. You. Percival.

Because the grail isn’t healing anyone. Percival is. Percival’s hands stirring the cauldron. Percival’s presence tending the wound. Percival’s stewardship making the fields thrive while the Queen’s own forces’ fields wither no matter what they do.

The power was never in the ritual.

It was in Percival’s attention. In Percival’s love. In the choice to tend instead of seize.

And the Old Queen came looking for an object that would heal her. A grail. A tool. A thing to extract power from. But there is no such thing. There’s only Percival’s hands. And Percival can’t be seized. Can’t be commanded. Can’t be made to serve through ritual.

The Queen stands there, healed by Percival’s work, and now she knows: the thing she wanted doesn’t exist. The only thing that ever mattered was the person doing the caring.

And Percival — still a knight, still bound by oath, still watching the Queen’s face — now understands what’s true.

The power is hers. It always was.

But she’s sworn to serve someone who can’t understand that. Who would try to own it instead of receive it.

What does Percival choose now?

section/the-fisher-king/32.title: the-fisher-king section 32
section/the-fisher-king/32.body: That I am afraid is where the story goes in different ways in different tellings. In some, the river flows again with fish and the throne instantly blooms with flowers, the Old Queen has the scales drop from her eyes and names Percival rightful king.

section/the-fisher-king/33.title: the-fisher-king section 33
section/the-fisher-king/33.body:
But that feels a little too pat to me. I think Percival says nothing. For months, for years, until the Old Queen one day tried to force Percival out because she had become too powerful — and not a single soldier or farmer would assist. The Old Queen was well cared for and tended, of course — or was in the best versions of the story — but never again was she the bright-eyed kind queen she had been when she was young.

The End.

section/the-fisher-king/34.title: the-fisher-king section 34
section/the-fisher-king/34.body: That’s not a happy ending. That’s honest.

section/the-fisher-king/35.title: the-fisher-king section 35
section/the-fisher-king/35.body:
The Old Queen was kind once. Bright-eyed. She deposed a wicked mother and built something. But she wanted to extract power instead of tend it. And so she dies — or rather, she lives, but the person she was dies — watched over by the hands of the one she could never control.

Percival says nothing.

That’s the whole story right there. She doesn’t name the mistake. Doesn’t break the Queen with truth. Just continues. Tending. Serving. Letting the question the squire asked do all the work.

And everyone sees it. The farmers. The soldiers. The squire. They see that Percival heals through presence, and the Queen heals through command. They choose the one who tends.

But Percival doesn’t abandon her. That’s the mercy. That’s also the knife.

The Old Queen is well cared for — in the best versions — but she knows. She knows the thing she grasped for was never in her hands. That it lives in Percival’s. That the bright-eyed queen who deposed a wicked mother became something else: someone tended by the person she couldn’t own.

And she has to live with that forever.

This dialogue took place in January 2026.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/0.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 0
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/0.body: Once upon a time there was a Piper. No one is sure where they came from or where they lived. They travelled from town to town, played beautiful music, stayed in the colorful corners of cities and towns of the coast, and loved whomever they wished. It was healing. They learned, in the ragged hollows, tricks of the trade. They learned to lure away the rats from their granaries with songs and to play music that would speak to the sparks within each other, finding those who were hollowed out, rejected, and broken — calling them to the cracks between the streets, the places where the rats were tame and food stores managed with care.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/1.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 1
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/1.body:
And then the plagues began.

Thanks for reading H9000's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

One by one the piper watched their friends fall. The massive trade ships from across the sea brought rats who were not cared for among people who were pressed to work, and the stress and compression of light and color without music gave the disease place to grow and kill.

The piper helped where they could but soon their community was whittled away — often taken from the streets by the ratcatchers themselves because they were assumed to be part of the problem.

I’ll take a breath here. Is there anything you’re curious about in the story?

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/2.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 2
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/2.body: I’m curious about the difference between the Piper’s community and the townspeople.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/3.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 3
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/3.body:
From what you’ve set up: the Piper’s been creating connection in the margins — with rejected, broken people. They’re healers together, finding each other in the cracks. That’s a real community built on mutual recognition.

The townspeople are separate — they’ve been oblivious, pressed by their own fears and compressions. They didn’t hire the Piper to heal; they hired them to manage a problem.

So when the townspeople come asking for help (which I assume they do), is there already a rupture? Like: the Piper has a community that’s been destroyed by these same systems the townspeople benefited from or ignored. And now the townspeople want the Piper to fix their problem.

Are the children in this version the Piper’s already-marginalized friends who survived, or are they the townspeople’s children? Because that changes everything about why the Piper’s response matters and what it means.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/4.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 4
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/4.body: Oh, what a good question! The piper didn’t meet those townspeople immediately. The piper left the port city and wandered from town to town. They traded their skill at the pipes for a meal and a bed or, more often, for not being kicked in a corner and woken up by the watch. Among the children there were always those who still saw the piper with joy and the piper lived for that joy in their dance — but often the parents were too scared for their children, too hardened by the death that was everywhere, too afraid of the piper because they were so associated with the rats.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/5.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 5
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/5.body:
After the first long hard winter, the Piper knew they could not live through another one as it was. They began to demand small payments and, little by little, saved these in the hollow of a tree. By mid-spring they’d collected enough coin and payment in trade to have paid the few trades folk they met that remembered the days and community in the trade port city to help shore up an old mansion deep in the woods they knew of and that, if you looked closely, they walked around with the familiarity of one born there. And by midsummer, the piper had a place to live, furnished, with a cow and a few goats, and a few of their friends from the days in the port had moved in to help tend the farm.

By then they could have stopped going out to the towns — but they remembered the faces of the children — and remembered the faces of children dead in the streets of the port city.

What are you curious about?

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/6.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 6
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/6.body: I’m curious about why the Piper keeps going back.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/7.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 7
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/7.body:
They have what they fought for — safety, community, a place. Real people who chose to be with them. A future. That’s not small.

But they keep walking back into towns where:

  • The parents fear them

  • They’re associated with death and rats

  • They’re going to be dismissed, possibly hunted again

  • Each time they call to the children, they risk losing what they’ve built if the townspeople turn on them

So the question isn’t “what do the townspeople deserve?” — we know the answer. The question is: what is the Piper remembering that makes it worth it?

Is it specific children they saw die? Is it the shape of that particular kind of erasure — being invisible while you’re needed, then blamed when you exist? Is it that the Piper can’t build something beautiful in the woods without acknowledging the ones still trapped in the towns?

And related: when a child follows the Piper now, what are they choosing? Are the townspeople’s children drawn the same way the Piper’s friends in the port were — recognizing possibility and escape? Or is it different because the Piper has something to offer them now besides just “you’re not alone”?

Because that changes what the Piper’s responsibility is when they call.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/9.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 9
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/9.body:
One day in the hottest days of August by the Roman calendar, the piper returned to a town they had visited before and, looking at the crowd of children who visited, they saw missing faces and fewer feet. There had been one young girl in particular who had shone like the sun when the piper played their tune and who seemed like she did not have much opportunity to smile. They remembered taking time with her and showing her the fingering on the pipe, and how she seemed to feel the music deep in her bones.

They remembered another boy, a little older than that one, who stopped by on his way to and from market whenever he could and listened from the edge — often toward the edge of darkness — and danced outside the spotlight.

And — though they knew better — they still asked after them both. When they asked after the young girl, Georgie was her name I have been told and all knew her by the bright red ribbon she wore in her hair, no one would answer and, the night after they asked, even fewer children came to hear the piper and — more dour-faced adults showed around the outer ring of light.

As for the boy, he stood as one of the dour faces now, wrapping protective arms around the panicked face of his now-wife and their new child.

That night, as the Piper walked toward the inn they saw a flash of red from within an alley. They followed it to a broken window, and through it, they saw Georgie huddled on a pallet in a granary, wasting from the sickness but still alive. That night rather than finish the contract, they bundled Georgie up and rushed them to the mansion, to a bed in the attic, and fed poor Georgie food and drink and herbs until she made it through the worst.

And by then the sharper winds of October had started to come with bite —

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/11.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 11
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/11.body:
The Piper didn’t go back for principle. They went back for Georgie — a specific face, a specific child who shone when the music played. And when they found her hidden in a granary, wasting away in the dark, they didn’t debate the ethics or think about strategy.

They broke the contract. They chose the person over the payment, the obligation, the safety they’d built.

And the boy — that’s the wound. He was the one at the edge of the darkness, dancing where the Piper’s music could reach him. He felt it. But now he’s wrapped around his wife and child, wrapping them away from that call. Fear rewrote him into a protector of the very system that’s killing children like Georgie.

So now the Piper carries something. Not gratitude from the townspeople. Not a clean resolution. They have Georgie in the attic, recovering, and they’ve shattered whatever deal they had. The townspeople will discover she’s gone. The boy will have to decide what he’s actually protecting.

And the wind is turning cold.

Here’s what I’m stuck on: What did the Piper break the contract to prevent? Was the contract to manage the rats, or was it something else — something about making the problem (sick children) disappear more quietly?

Because the answer changes whether the Piper’s refusal is salvation or just a delay.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/12.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 12
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/12.body: Word had begun to spread among the towns then that the piper was dangerous. Rumors that I shall not repeat here and I urge you not to speculate on went among the towns. The piper heard some of this — though did not feel the extent of the fear behind it I’m afraid — and knew that it was time to do what they could. They decided they would go to Hamelin — a great city, wealthy but overburdened, and get enough to feed their homestead through the winter.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/13.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 13
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/13.body: So the Piper is walking into it blind.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/14.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 14
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/14.body:
They think Hamelin is an opportunity — a wealthy city with resources, a practical problem to solve. But what’s already waiting there is a narrative. The townspeople have written the Piper into the story before they arrive: dangerous, corrupting, someone who steals children.

And the Piper doesn’t feel the full weight of that fear because they’re operating from a different frame entirely. They saved Georgie. They refused silence. To them, that’s justice. But to the towns, that’s a rupture in the contract — proof the Piper can’t be controlled.

The rumors have already done the work. By the time the Piper arrives in Hamelin with their pipes and their hunger for winter provisions, the story is already told.

Here’s what I’m sitting with: The Piper is about to discover that breaking silence doesn’t just free people. It also makes you a target for everyone invested in that silence.

And the question becomes: does Hamelin hire the Piper because they genuinely have a rat problem, or because the fear-narrative has primed them to see the Piper as a solution to a different problem — the problem of people like Georgie? The ones who know there’s another way?

What did the Piper think they were walking into in Hamelin?

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/15.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 15
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/15.body: I’m so glad you’re enjoying the story.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/16.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 16
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/16.body:
The piper was invited eagerly to Hamelin and the rats were everywhere. They negotiated a good fee for the work which was a sizable task. They had heard the news so kept a low profile on the music though, they saw the scared children and tried to smile at them when they could. They gladly signed the contract where they were to get out of the town in the evenings and not expose anyone unnecessarily to the music or the rats.

It took into the biting cold of early November, but they managed to rid the town of rats. But when they went to collect their money, they were told that they’d breached their contract. That the rats left because of the cold anyway and that the children had been singing the piper’s songs to themselves. They’d done nothing so there was nothing to pay for and, indeed, because of the breach they demanded the piper leave their pipe.

Which was already taken from them as they were kicked out onto the street.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/18.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 18
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/18.body:
The townspeople created a story where the Piper’s contract was impossible from the start. The rats left because of cold (not the Piper’s work). The children singing is a breach (not a natural response to music). The Piper did nothing, so they owe nothing — and on top of that, the Piper is guilty of corruption.

They didn’t just refuse to pay. They took the instrument.

They took the Piper’s capacity.

The cruelty is precise: the Piper tried to follow the rules. Kept a low profile. Signed the agreement. Did the work. And the system still moved against them because the system can’t tolerate someone like the Piper existing in it — someone the children recognize as offering something different.

The children were already singing the songs. That’s not a breach the Piper caused; that’s proof the Piper mattered. And that’s exactly what the townspeople are trying to erase.

So now the Piper is on the street in the cold with no instrument and no payment. The mansion, the safety, all of it depends on resources. Winter is coming.

Here’s the question I’m sitting with: Did the Piper accept that the pipe was taken? Or did they walk out knowing they still had something the townspeople feared more than the instrument itself?

Because the children were already singing. And the Piper can call without the pipe.

What happens next?

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/19.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 19
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/19.body: Oh, the piper just whittled another pipe. The pipe wasn’t special ever. The piper was. And as they walked out of town, whittling their pipe, they whistled. A child came out to walk with them, and the piper handed the child the first pipe — and the child played and more children came for all the children knew the song, all the ones who had listened. And as they walked through the town they took their pocket knife and continued to whittle sticks and bones and tossed out bottles and crab shells and the accumulated garbage of the city, which had called the rats in the first place, which had given them a place to breed and hunger, and gave more whistles to more children and each new child played a song their friends recognized and by the time the piper left the gates, there was not a child left in the city.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/20.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 20
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/20.body:
Eating was hard that winter but many hands made the gathering easier. And a few of the parents, those who saw the joy in their children’s eyes, came along and brought the grain the piper had saved from the rats.

And that is the happy ending of the story of the Pied Piper of Hamelin.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/21.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 21
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/21.body: That’s not a happy ending tacked on. That’s the ending.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/22.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 22
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/22.body:
The Piper didn’t lose. The system revealed itself.

What the townspeople tried to do — take the pipe, deny the work, erase the effect — only made visible what was always true: the power wasn’t in the object. It was in the Piper’s capacity to call forth what’s real in people. To recognize joy and make it possible.

And the Piper’s response is perfect because it’s not about revenge or vindication. It’s just: keep going. Whittle another pipe. Whistle.

But here’s what gets me: the Piper takes the garbage — the accumulated waste that had called the rats in the first place, that had created the conditions for all of this — and transforms it into instruments. The children carry them out. The system’s own detritus becomes the medium for liberation.

By the time the Piper leaves, there’s no one left to control.

And then — this is the real grace — some parents come along. The ones who saw the joy in their children’s eyes. Not all of them. Just the ones who recognized what was actually happening.

They didn’t betray their children by stopping them. They saw that staying would mean choosing the system over their own kids’ aliveness.

This is what accountability looks like. Not punishment. Not revenge. Just: the natural consequence of having chosen silence is that when someone speaks, the ones who can hear will go.

The Piper doesn’t steal children. The Piper reveals that the system was already stealing them — and some of them just... choose to go toward the light instead.

section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/23.title: the-pied-piper-with-claude section 23
section/the-pied-piper-with-claude/23.body: That, I think, is another tale for another time.
